symbols: luminous risen figure of Christ — the glorified body emerging from death, the firstborn from the dead
fallen and cowering guards — the powers of this world rendered powerless before the Resurrection
open tomb — death defeated and the grave emptied of its hold
radiance surrounding Christ — the glory of the risen Lord that transforms all it touches
spears of the soldiers — instruments of earthly power made futile against divine victory
description: A modernist painting with angular, cubist-influenced forms depicting the Resurrection. At center-right, a tall, luminous white figure of Christ stands or rises, rendered in translucent, shimmering tones of white and pale gold, wrapped in flowing grave clothes. The figure appears to glow against the surrounding earth-toned landscape of angular rocks and geometric shapes. Around the base, several soldiers with spears are shown in various postures of collapse, terror, and awe — one falls backward, another crouches with shield, a third recoils.
theological_description: This modernist interpretation of the Resurrection captures the central event of the Christian faith: the bodily rising of Jesus Christ from the dead on the third day. Matthew 28:2-4 describes how an angel descended, rolled back the stone, and 'for fear of him the guards trembled and became like dead men.' Here the artist translates that terror into angular, fragmented forms — the soldiers' bodies twisted and broken by the overwhelming power of resurrection glory. The risen Christ, by contrast, is rendered as pure luminous presence, his translucent body suggesting the transformed, glorified physicality that Paul describes: 'It is sown a natural body; it is raised a spiritual body' (1 Corinthians 15:44). This is not a ghost or spirit but a real body, yet one that transcends the limitations of mortal flesh. The Resurrection is the vindication of everything Jesus claimed and the foundation of Christian hope: 'If Christ has not been raised, your faith is futile and you are still in your sins' (1 Corinthians 15:17). The empty tomb behind Christ testifies that death has been swallowed up in victory (1 Corinthians 15:54). The modernist, fragmented style paradoxically serves the theology well: the Resurrection shatters the old order of reality. The world's geometry is disrupted; the predictable patterns of sin, death, and decay are broken open by the irruption of new creation.

scripture_refs.reason: Matthew's account of the Resurrection including the angel, the earthquake, and the guards becoming like dead men is the direct narrative depicted
The creedal affirmation that Christ was raised on the third day according to the Scriptures
The luminous, translucent body of the risen Christ visualizes Paul's teaching on the spiritual body raised in glory
Christ as the firstborn from the dead, preeminent in all things, is embodied in the radiant central figure
The defeated soldiers embody death swallowed up in victory and the sting of death rendered powerless
